content.short_summary: Metformin is a biguanide medicine used to help manage Type 2 diabetes as part of an individual care plan.
why_it_is_used: Metformin may be used alone or with other medicines to help manage Type 2 diabetes.
It is one of several treatment options a healthcare team may consider based on a person’s needs.
how_it_works: Metformin lowers the amount of glucose made by the liver and helps the body respond better to insulin.
It is an educational description, not a prediction of an individual response.
common_benefits: May help lower blood glucose when it is prescribed as part of a Type 2 diabetes care plan.
May be used with other diabetes medicines when a clinician determines that combination is appropriate.
common_side_effects: Stomach-related effects such as diarrhea, nausea, stomach discomfort, gas, indigestion, or constipation can occur.
Side effects and their severity vary; a pharmacist or healthcare professional can help with questions.
important_considerations: Research-backed editorial draft. Not yet reviewed by a qualified clinician. Must not be published.
Kidney health and other medical factors can matter when a healthcare team considers metformin.
Rare but serious lactic acidosis is an important safety warning; urgent or severe symptoms need professional care.
Medication choices are individual. This is general education, not a reason to start, stop, or change a medicine based on this app.
questions_for_healthcare_team: What is metformin intended to help with in my care plan?
Which side effects should I discuss with my pharmacist or healthcare team?
What health conditions, procedures, or other medicines should my team know about?
content.key_takeaway: Metformin is a common Type 2 diabetes medicine, but whether it is suitable and how it is used are decisions for an individual and their healthcare team.
